ontology.foodex2_description: Chicken meat, grilled — used as closest defensible FoodEx2 anchor for marinated grilled chicken skewer preparation
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: No FoodEx2 code exists specifically for Satay Ayam as a composite dish; A0FJP (grilled chicken) used as the primary anchor. Peanut sauce component not separately coded.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for Satay Ayam as a specific food concept.

allergens: gluten (possible from soy sauce in marinade)

serving.description: 1 serving (approximately 5 skewers with peanut sauce and accompaniments)
serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of Satay Ayam consists of approximately 5 chicken skewers (around 150–170 g of chicken) served with peanut sauce, ketupat, sliced cucumber, and raw onion, totalling approximately 250 g.

nutrient_source.title: Satay, chicken — HPB Singapore Foodo / IMR Malaysia MyFCD composite reference for grilled marinated chicken with peanut sauce
nutrient_source.organization: Health Promotion Board (HPB) Singapore; Institute for Medical Research (IMR) Malaysia MyFCD
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Satay Ayam values cross-referenced between HPB Singapore Foodo (chicken satay entry) and IMR Malaysia MyFCD (sate ayam entry). Input AI-generated values were validated against these two primary regional sources. Core macros and micronutrients are consistent with HPB/MyFCD data for grilled marinated chicken satay with peanut sauce at approximately 185 kcal per 100 g. Saturated fat estimated from HPB chicken satay entry. Monounsaturated, polyunsaturated, trans fat, cholesterol, and water data not separately reported in these sources and set to -1.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values — chicken satay / grilled marinated chicken with peanut sauce analogous estimate
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. Diabetes Care 2008
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for Satay Ayam exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 45 (low) is an analogous estimate based on: (1) grilled marinated chicken has negligible GI contribution; (2) peanut sauce GI ~14 (peanuts, University of Sydney GI Database); (3) ketupat (compressed rice) GI ~65 but constitutes a minor fraction of the composite dish as served. Composite dish GI estimated at 45 (low), consistent with high protein and fat content moderating glycaemic response. GL per serving = (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (45 × 17.0) / 100 = 7.65 ≈ 7.7. Carbohydrate per 250 g serving = 6.8 × 2.5 = 17.0 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content 320 mg per 100 g; a 250 g serving delivers approximately 800 mg sodium, representing about 35% of the 2300 mg daily limit.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to 2–3 skewers and request reduced peanut sauce, as the marinade and sauce together contribute the majority of sodium in this dish.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index (estimated GI 45) and moderate carbohydrate content (6.8 g per 100 g); high protein (14.5 g) and fat (11.2 g) further blunt postprandial glucose response.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Satay Ayam is generally suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when ketupat portion is controlled; the peanut sauce contains 4.5 g sugar per 100 g and should be used in moderation to avoid cumulative sugar load.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat approximately 3.1 g per 100 g and sodium 320 mg per 100 g; charcoal grilling may produce heterocyclic amines (HCAs) and polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons (PAHs) at high temperatures.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should moderate portion size, choose lean chicken breast skewers where possible, limit peanut sauce quantity, and be aware that frequent consumption of charcoal-grilled meats is associated with increased cardiovascular risk due to HCA and PAH formation.

peanut_allergy.key_factor: Peanut sauce (kuah kacang) is a core component of the dish.
peanut_allergy.guidance: Satay Ayam must be avoided entirely by individuals with peanut allergy; cross-contamination risk is high in street food settings.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Marinade contains turmeric (curcumin) and galangal with documented anti-inflammatory properties.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Satay Ayam may be consumed by individuals with rheumatoid arthritis; turmeric and galangal in the marinade provide anti-inflammatory benefit, though frequent charcoal-grilled meat consumption should be moderated due to AGE formation.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Satay Ayam (Chicken Satay) is one of the most iconic dishes of Malay, Indonesian, and Singaporean cuisine. It consists of small pieces of chicken — typically thigh or breast meat — marinated in a fragrant spice blend of lemongrass, turmeric, galangal, shallots, garlic, and coriander seeds, then threaded onto bamboo skewers and grilled over a charcoal fire. The charcoal grilling imparts a characteristic smoky, slightly charred flavour to the tender, juicy chicken. The dish is traditionally served with kuah kacang (a rich, spicy peanut sauce made from ground roasted peanuts, coconut milk, lemongrass, galangal, and palm sugar), ketupat (compressed rice cakes wrapped in woven palm leaves), sliced fresh cucumber, and raw shallots or onion rings. Satay Ayam is widely consumed as a street food snack, party appetiser, or main meal component across Malaysia, Singapore, and Indonesia. Nutritionally, a 100 g serving provides approximately 185 kcal, 14.5 g protein, 11.2 g fat, and 6.8 g carbohydrate, making it a protein-rich, moderate-fat dish with a low glycaemic index (estimated GI 45). The sodium content of 320 mg per 100 g warrants attention for individuals managing hypertension. The dish is halal and dairy-free but contains peanuts (major allergen). Clinically, it is suitable for Type 2 Diabetes management when ketupat and peanut sauce portions are controlled, and is moderate concern for cardiovascular disease due to saturated fat and sodium from the sauce and marinade.

Saturated fat (3.1 g per 100 g) estimated from HPB Singapore Foodo chicken satay entry. 3. GI of 45 is an analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database peanut and grilled chicken data; no direct GI measurement for Satay Ayam exists in approved GI sources. 4. Glycemic load per serving calculated as (45 × 17.0 g carbohydrate per 250 g serving) / 100 = 7.7. 5. The dish is widely attributed to both Indonesian and Malay culinary traditions; country_of_origin is noted as Southeast Asia broadly. 6. Peanut allergy is a significant concern; this dish should never be served to individuals with peanut allergy. 7. All core macros (protein_g 14.5, fat_g 11.2, carbohydrate_g 6.8) are non-zero and nutritionally consistent with a grilled marinated chicken dish with peanut sauce.
